grader_components.part: the same cohort overestimated improvements in climate technology
it was the SAME cohort that made the AI error (simultaneity/linkage)
"the opposite directional error" (overestimation vs. the AI underestimation)

grader_proofs_verified: Key findings include: (a) there was overall performance parity between superforecasters and domain experts, with both groups underestimating AI progress and overestimating improvements in climate technology
grader_why: The shown sentences prove only that the cohort overestimated climate-technology progress; they say nothing about AI, so they cannot establish the claim's core point — that this was the SAME cohort's error in the OPPOSITE direction from its AI underestimation. That contrast is fully provable from the source's key-findings sentence, which states both errors together for both groups, but the tool never displayed it. Proof exists in the source but is not among the shown sentences, so the tool must fetch it.
fable_reasoning: Components: same cohort overestimated climate tech (proven verbatim), and this is the 'opposite directional error' vs AI (flagged uncovered). Arbiter (wrong_or_insufficient_evidence) states the AI-vs-climate contrast exists in the source abstract but was not shown. The directional-contrast is interpretive framing built on two source facts both present -> supported; amber is a display miss.

grader_components.part: Superforecasters earned their reputation in domains built on recurring structure: elections, GDP prints, conflict escalation, disease spread
Geopolitical forecasting is an 'algorithm-unfriendly' domain — hard-to-quantify inputs, elusive reference classes
trained human forecasters, especially when aggregated, hold their own against statistical models in geopolitics
research on clinical vs statistical prediction shows algorithms make more accurate predictions than humans in many domains

grader_missing_subclaim: Superforecasters earned their reputation in domains built on recurring structure: elections, GDP prints, conflict escalation, disease spread
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Geopolitical forecasting is an algorithm-unfriendly domain with hard-to-quantify data and elusive reference classes, so although research on clinical versus statistical prediction shows algorithms beat humans in many domains, forecasting here is typically done by humans aided by algorithms [mellers2023].
grader_proofs_verified: Research on clinical versus statistical prediction has demonstrated that algorithms make more accurate predictions than humans in many domains.
Geopolitical forecasting is an algorithm-unfriendly domain, with hard-to-quantify data and elusive reference classes that make predictive model-building difficult.
grader_why: This is a mixed case, so the author's problem dominates. The lead claim that superforecasters 'earned their reputation in domains built on recurring structure: elections, GDP prints, conflict escalation, disease spread' is nowhere in the source — indeed the source frames geopolitical forecasting as having elusive reference classes, the opposite of recurring structure — and the interpretive 'hold their own against statistical models' comparison is also not stated (the authors note they lacked statistical models for most tournament questions). The two abstract components (clinical-vs-statistical superiority and the algorithm-unfriendly characterization) ARE in the source and are quoted for fetching, but the fetchable part alone cannot rescue the unsourced superforecaster-domain subclaim.
fable_reasoning: Conjunction: (a) superforecasters earned their reputation in recurring-structure domains (elections/GDP/conflict/disease), (b) trained humans hold their own vs statistical models in geopolitical forecasting, (c) clinical-vs-statistical: algorithms beat humans in many domains, (d) geopolitical forecasting is algorithm-unfriendly. Source proves only the background (c)+(d); the two distinctive assertions (a),(b) are absent. Centrality guard: only framing proven -> unsupported. Arbiter concurs (add_citation_or_rewrite).

grader_components.part: Tournament held in 2022
Named the Existential Risk Persuasion Tournament
169 superforecasters and domain experts
forecast benchmarks/questions resolving by 2024–2025

grader_proofs_verified: This report analyzes respondents’ forecasting accuracy on 38 near-term questions that resolved by mid-2025.
Participants in the tournament forecast on questions set to resolve at various dates ranging from as early as mid-2024 to as late as 2100.
The tournament included 59 forecasting questions set to resolve at dates ranging from mid-2024 to as late as 2100.
grader_why: The shown sentences prove the year (2022), the tournament name (XPT), and the 169 superforecasters and domain experts, but every displayed sentence describes the forecasts as about 'long-term risks' and 'humanity's long-term future' — none establishes the claim's 'benchmarks resolving by 2024–2025' component. That near-term-resolution component is provable elsewhere in kucinskas2025 (38 near-term questions resolved by mid-2025; questions set to resolve from mid-2024 onward), so the proof exists in the source but was not among the shown sentences. Per rule B, 'provable nearby' is a tool problem, not 'supported.'
fable_reasoning: Components: 2022 XPT (proven), 169 superforecasters+experts (proven verbatim; 80+89=169 corroborated by karger2023), benchmarks resolving 2024-2025 (flagged uncovered). Arbiter (wrong_or_insufficient_evidence) confirms the resolution-window proof EXISTS in both sources (karger2023 end-of-2024, kucinskas2025 mid-2024 onward) but was not displayed. All components provable -> supported; the partial/amber is a retrieval-display artifact, not a real gap.

grader_components.part: AI experts had similar medians
AI experts overconfident in the tails
superforecasters systematically underpredicted progress
generalist crowd forecasters fared worst

grader_proofs_verified: They had similar medians to me but were (probably) overconfident in the tails.
The superforecasters from Karger et al. did the next best. They (probably) systematically underpredicted progress.
The forecasters from Hypermind did the worst. They underpredicted progress significantly on MMLU.
In this case, it seems the experts did pretty well and better than generalist forecasters.
grader_why: Each component is proven within the shown sentences, including the 'Displayed reading view': the AI experts' similar medians and tail-overconfidence, the superforecasters' systematic underprediction, and Hypermind (the commissioned crowd/'generalist forecasters') doing the worst. The claim's implied ranking (experts > superforecasters > crowd) matches the source's stated ordering, and the source itself uses the term 'generalist forecasters.'
fable_reasoning: Three factual components: AI experts similar medians/overconfident tails (proven), superforecasters underpredicted (arbiter: sentence exists, not shown), generalist crowd worst (arbiter quotes 'The forecasters from Hypermind did the worst'). All three provable in Steinhardt -> the factual claim is supported; the 'independent evidence points the same way' framing is the author's synthesis but the underlying facts hold.

grader_components.part: median expert substantially underestimating SOTA AI progress
FRI's ongoing expert panel (LEAP, longitudinal)
shows this PERSISTING (continuity from prior findings)
into 2025-2026 (the 2025 survey-window anchor)

grader_proofs_verified: Every month, LEAP tracks the views of top AI scientists, industry leaders, policy researchers, economists, and superforecasters on the trajectory of the development and use of artificial intelligence.
This post covers key highlights from the Wave 4 LEAP survey that we conducted between November 20 and December 20, 2025.
Experts’ underestimation of benchmark progress is also evident in earlier work from FRI.
In a paper released in September 2025, we found that domain experts and superforecasters underestimated AI progress across the MATH, MMLU, and QuaLITY benchmarks.
grader_why: The core sentence about the median expert underestimating SOTA AI progress is shown verbatim, and the panel is identified as LEAP Wave 4. But 'persisting' asserts continuity over time, whose proof ('also evident in earlier work from FRI… a paper released in September 2025') is in the source yet not displayed, and the '2025' end of the 2025-2026 window (survey conducted Nov-Dec 2025) is likewise provable from the source but unshown. The author's framing is accurate and fetchable, so this is a tool display gap, not an author fix.
fable_reasoning: Core assertion (median expert substantially underestimating SOTA AI progress) proven verbatim. The 2025-2026 timeframe was flagged uncovered, but arbiter (action=supported) confirms the shown Wave-4 panel is dated Jan 12 2026 (=2025-2026). Every component covered -> supported; partial flag spurious.

grader_components.part: the structural problem is reference-class reasoning
superforecasting leans on outside-view base rates from many similar past events
elections and wars recur, GDP mean-reverts
AI capability growth is discontinuous
AI capability growth is compute-driven
AI leaves no comparable historical baseline

grader_missing_subclaim: the characterization of superforecasting as outside-view base-rate/reference-class reasoning grounded in recurring events (elections and wars recurring, GDP mean-reverting) — the source never describes how superforecasting works, and never mentions elections, wars, or GDP; it also never states AI growth is 'compute-driven' or that AI 'leaves no comparable historical baseline'
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Forecasting AI progress is harder than forecasting other technologies, in part because AI progress can be discontinuous and there are few prior forecasts to validate methods against [gruetzemacher2021].
grader_proofs_verified: One particular challenge in modeling AI progress is modeling discontinuous progress.
These issues make it more challenging to model AI progress in the same way as other technologies.
However, there are not many data points to evaluate methods within AI since not that many proper forecasts have been carried out.
grader_why: The claim's core contrast — that superforecasting relies on outside-view base rates from recurring events (elections, wars, GDP mean-reversion) while AI lacks such a reference class — rests on characterizations and examples the source never states; the source discusses AI forecasting difficulty but never describes superforecasting's mechanism, never mentions elections/wars/GDP, and never says AI growth is 'compute-driven' or lacks a 'historical baseline.' Only the 'discontinuous' component is provable from the source (and it is not among the shown sentences), while the interpretive reference-class thesis and the non-AI examples are author additions requiring their own citation or a rewrite. Because unprovable components dominate, this is the author's problem, not merely a display problem.
fable_reasoning: Pipeline flipped to supported via tail_rescue, but the shown evidence sentence is irrelevant and covering.uncovered lists ALL FOUR components. Arbiter (add_citation_or_rewrite): source never mentions reference-class reasoning, outside-view base rates, elections/wars/GDP, nor states AI growth is discontinuous/compute-driven with no baseline. Nothing substantive proven -> unsupported. The reference-class thesis is the author's own analytical framing.

grader_components.part: existential AI risk dominated by epistemic rather than aleatoric uncertainty
epistemic = ignorance/unknown mechanism; aleatoric = stable random process
standard probability elicitation not built to represent this ignorance
probability elicitation is 'the superforecaster's core tool'

grader_missing_subclaim: that standard probability elicitation is 'the superforecaster's core tool'
grader_rewrite_suggestion: One reading is that existential AI risk is dominated by epistemic uncertainty (a lack of knowledge) rather than aleatoric uncertainty (randomness) — exactly the kind of ignorance that standard probability elicitation was not built to represent.
grader_proofs_verified: In AI risk, rather than in dice rolls, ignorance is the dominant form of uncertainty, not randomness, so the best techniques are not always probabilistic.

Those additional questions will force policymakers, experts, and analysts to grapple with the epistemic uncertainty that dominates AI risk more directly , and to avoid conflating epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties the way point estimates and even probability distributions do.
A recent review of relevant risk literature noted that “it is difficult to deal with epistemic uncertainty effectively only through probability theory.
grader_why: The core thesis (AI risk is dominated by epistemic over aleatoric uncertainty, which probability elicitation is ill-suited to represent) is fully proven by the shown sentences. But the appositive characterizing probability elicitation as 'the superforecaster's core tool' is not in the source, which mentions superforecasters only as producing a single likelihood estimate and never frames probability as their core tool. That unsourced characterization is the author's to fix by rewrite or added citation.
fable_reasoning: Core reading (existential AI risk dominated by epistemic vs aleatoric uncertainty; probability ill-suited to that ignorance) is supported by the source. The appositive 'the superforecaster's core tool is standard probability elicitation' is not in lohn2026 (arbiter confirms). Core proven + one characterization unsourced -> partial; pipeline keeps supported but ambers the same component, so both surface the gap.
